pages.text:
1

HOW TO FILL IN YOUR MICROS
headings: HOW TO FILL IN YOUR MICROS

pages.text:
2

The current fitness culture emphasizes macronutrients with little regard for
micronutrients, not to mention phytochemicals and other compounds that can have
significant effects on your health or performance. To make sure you cover all your
micronutrient needs, here's an overview of the most common micronutrient deficiencies
and which foods are semi-essential to consume enough of them. The last column
describes an effective supplementation strategy for when the diet does not provide
adequate amounts of the micronutrient. The table is not exhaustive and there are many
ways to cover your micronutrient needs, but in practice many people will require either
supplementation or the listed food sources to avoid deficiency.

For more information, see the online Henselmans PT Course module on micronutrition
or ask your coach.

Micronutrient
Good dietary sources
Supplementation
strategy
Vitamin K
• Leafy green vegetables
• Avocado
• Pumpkin
• Natto
1+ mcg/kg/d
phylloquinones (vitamin
K1) consumed with a fatty
meal
Calcium
• Dairy
• Whole sardines
• Cruciferous vegetables in very
large amounts
800 mg/d calcium (any
form) consumed with a
low-magnesium meal
Vitamin D
• Sun
2-9000 IU/d Vitamin D3
with bloodwork done to
maintain serum 25-
hydroxyvitamin D levels of
~115 nmol/l
Magnesium
• Spinach
• Mackerel
Up to 6 mg/kg/d
magnesium citrate,

3

• Fermented/soaked/sprouted
whole grains (oats, wheat, rice,
nuts, beans, cacao/pure
chocolate)
• Potato
chloride, lactate or
aspartate (nót oxide)
consumed with a low-
calcium meal
Iodine
• Iodized salt
• Seaweed
• Dairy in large amounts
150+ mcg/d iodine from
standardized kelp extract
Zinc
• Red meat
• Shellfish
10-15 mg/d zinc (any
form) consumed with a
low-calcium and low-
magnesium meal
Iron
• Dark red meats, incl. beef
• Organ meats
• Certain types of shellfish

Risky! Have bloodwork
done and consult with
your coach or a medical
professional.

HOW TO FILL IN YOUR MICROS


--- Page 2 ---

The current fitness culture emphasizes macronutrients with little regard for
micronutrients, not to mention phytochemicals and other compounds that can have
significant effects on your health or performance. To make sure you cover all your
micronutrient needs, here's an overview of the most common micronutrient deficiencies

For more information, see the online Henselmans PT Course module on micronutrition
or ask your coach.
